storyname: The search for a new mother
aboutcontents:
I was the happiest kid in preschool! Whenever one of my grandmother's enchanted stories was projected on the class wall, we would close the window blinds, huddle together on the carpet, and sit completely mesmerised. It was a magical moment, an innocent moment, a classic childhood moment, which I craved to pass along to my children as well some day.
Grandma Adina, my grandmother,  was an extraordinary person. She seemed to know everything about plants and animals, and she loved children's stories. But most of all, she loved us, her grandchildren.
Grandma was a gifted painter, and Grandpa who admired her wanted her stories to reach as many children as possible. Together they opened a small workshop in their home, where Grandma would paint her stories, and Grandpa would turn them into slides and posters. Her works touched countless people in the 1950's and starred in kindergartens, schools, clinics and post offices.
On summer vacations my parents would take my sisters and I to Grandma and Grandpa's country home, where I would help slice the large rolls of film into story reels, and roll them ever so carefully on my skirt to avoid getting fingerprints on the film.
Today I'm all grown up. I look at grandma Adina's life work, and have a burning desire to share them with today's generation of children - and also with today's adults, who long for the innocence and simplicity of days past.
And so I embarked on this project - to take Grandma Adina's magical stories, turn them into an interactive storybook app while preserving the original simplicity and innocence.
I was very lucky to be able to finish at least one such story before Grandma Adina's passing - and there wasn't a happier person in the old people's home that day when I showed it to her.
Thank you Grandma Adina. I love you. Hanna
